subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Bystander Effect and Helping Behavior
stimulus:
Dr. Germanotta hypothesized that the more witnesses there are to a crime, the less likely any one witness will
help. She collected data on the number of people who helped someone in distress and the number of people
walking by at that particular time. Dr. Germanotta found the following statistically significant results.

parts.prompt: Explain how the data presented in the graph above support or do not support Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the data do not support Dr. Germanotta’s hypothesis,  because the relationship is a positive, rather than a negative one.

required_elements: The graph did not support the doctor’s hypothesis because as one variable increases, the other increases as well, and she predicted the opposite.
alternatives.correct_example: The graph did not support the doctor’s hypothesis because as one variable increases, the other increases as well, and she predicted the opposite.

parts.prompt: Identify the statistical measure that Dr. Germanotta would use to assess the strength of the relationship between variables.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the appropriate statistical measure is a correlation  coefficient (e.g., r, coefficient of determination, standardized beta weight, etc.).

required_elements: In order to measure the strength of the relationship between variables, she should use Pearson’s r.
alternatives.correct_example: In order to measure the strength of the relationship between variables, she should use Pearson’s r.

wrong_examples: Dr. Germanotta should perform a correlation.
Dr. Germanotta should perform a chi-square test for significance.

parts.prompt: Explain how the third variable problem could influence the results.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that there may be some other variable not in the study that  may have affected the two variables being studied. 

OR 

The response must indicate that the presence/influence of some other variable not in  the study would prevent Dr. Germanotta from drawing a cause-and-effect conclusion.

required_elements: There could have been a lot of people walking by for a particular reason, such as a protest. The protestors might be more concerned with the welfare of others than a person normally would be.
alternatives.correct_example: There could have been a lot of people walking by for a particular reason, such as a protest. The protestors might be more concerned with the welfare of others than a person normally would be.

required_elements: Dr. Germanotta cannot say that the number of people caused helping because there may have been people there because of a protest.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Germanotta cannot say that the number of people caused helping because there may have been people there because of a protest.

wrong_examples: Time of day could affect the results.
Type of crime could influence the results as participants may be more likely to help

parts.prompt: Dr. Germanotta decides to conduct an experiment to test whether the number of people present during a staged emergency situation will influence helping behavior. Identify the independent variable in this study.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate the independent variable is the number of people present.

required_elements: The independent variable in Dr. Germanotta’s experiment would be the number of people in the area.
alternatives.correct_example: The independent variable in Dr. Germanotta’s experiment would be the number of people in the area.

wrong_examples: The independent variable would be the amount of helping behaviors.

parts.prompt: Explain how Internal locus of control relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that if a person believes that their efforts will be helpful,  they will be more likely to help. 

OR 

The response must indicate that if they believe their efforts will not be helpful, they will  be less likely to help.

required_elements: People are more inclined to help others if they believe that it is within their power to actually make a real difference.
alternatives.correct_example: People are more inclined to help others if they believe that it is within their power to actually make a real difference.

required_elements: People are less likely to help if they think that their helping won’t matter.
alternatives.correct_example: People are less likely to help if they think that their helping won’t matter.

parts.prompt: Explain how Modeling relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that a person observes some behavior related to helping or  not helping, and then performs the same behavior.

required_elements: We learn a lot of helping behaviors growing up through observation. For example, a little kid might see their parents hold a door open for someone, so the next time they go to the store the kid holds the door open for the person behind them.
alternatives.correct_example: We learn a lot of helping behaviors growing up through observation. For example, a little kid might see their parents hold a door open for someone, so the next time they go to the store the kid holds the door open for the person behind them.

wrong_examples: One person sees another helping others, so they want to mimic that helpful behavior.
People learn helping behaviors through modeling.

parts.prompt: Explain how Approach-avoidance conflict relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate a decision-making process in which there is BOTH an  attractive and an unattractive aspect of helping.

required_elements: Sarah really wants to help out at the animal shelter because she loves dogs, but she is hesitant because she thinks cleaning the cages would be gross.
alternatives.correct_example: Sarah really wants to help out at the animal shelter because she loves dogs, but she is hesitant because she thinks cleaning the cages would be gross.
